problem_summary: 2018 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research): design a national/global rollout of EV charging stations. The team had to (1) judge whether Tesla's existing US Supercharger network is on track to support a full switch to all-electric and compute how many stations are needed at full electrification, (2) pick a country (they chose South Korea) and determine the number, location, and growth timeline of charging stations plus the key drivers, (3) decide whether a classification system can generalize the model across countries with different geography/population/wealth, (4) assess how new technologies (autonomy, hyperloop, fast-charging) affect EV adoption, and (5) produce a one-page handout for national leaders with a gas-vehicle-ban date.
models_used: Queuing theory M/M/1 single-server model per Supercharger station (Poisson arrivals, exponential service), computing queue length Lq = lambda^2/(mu*(mu-lambda)) and average wait Tq = Lq/lambda to size station capacity
Demand model n_i = rho_i * S_i * H_i * P_i (population density x area x cars-per-capita x EV penetration) to estimate actual EV count per region

Multi-objective integer programming for station count: minimize total build cost (Z = sum r_DC*C_DC + r_SC*C_SC) and minimize weighted average waiting time, subject to distance/charging-mile coverage, a convenience-index constraint CI<=1, an SC-to-DC efficiency-ratio relation r_SC = r_DC * P_efficiency, and integer/non-negativity constraints; solved in MATLAB

Urban site-selection model: an n x n weighted grid (concentric levels) with a shortest-path 0-1 network-flow formulation (flow-balance constraints), augmented by a custom dynamic congestion index that re-weights edges across iterations (w_jk = 1 + epsilon*(m-i)) so chosen sites spread out instead of clustering downtown; infinite edge weights for lakes/rivers/oceans/deserts
Suburban model: reduced to a highway/corridor station-spacing problem driven by traffic volume VF_ih

Rural model: geometric full-coverage via a triangular-mesh packing of service circles (radius = single-charge mileage), using Heron's formula for triangle area to guarantee area coverage at minimum cost

Logistic regression (logit, fitted in SAS via PROC LOGISTIC) on six candidate predictors (EVP, DP, WD, EA, PO, GI) to model EV growth rate p; only WD, PO, GI passed significance, yielding separate urban/rural/national regression equations and an adoption timeline

Q-type (Q-mode) hierarchical clustering of 8 countries on 5 indicators (DP, GDP, CA, GI, EVP), using Mahalanobis distance (to remove inter-indicator correlation/scale effects) with single-linkage (nearest-neighbor) agglomeration, producing 3 country categories
Logistic-growth (Verhelst) evaluation model r = K*R0*e^((GDP+URP+OPH)t) / (K + R0*(e^(...)-1)) on standardized technology indicators to test whether new technologies raise the EV growth rate
Custom 'lag index' epsilon = |x-y|/omega to quantify the temporal lag/coupling between charger build-out and EV sales curves
key_techniques: Urban-suburban-rural stratification by a population-density threshold (>150, 50-150, <150 per km^2) used as the organizing spine across every sub-question

Defining bespoke, named diagnostic indices to turn fuzzy requirements into computable quantities: 'connectivity rate' (fraction of adjacent stations within the 170-mile single-charge range, measured 71% for the US), 'convenience index' CI (demand vs. serviceable supply, with CI<1 => convenient), and 'lag index'

Grounding parameters in real data: scraped Tesla's published station coordinates (MATLAB string parsing of WebData.txt) and Supercharger growth from supercharge.info, US Census population density, GM's 35% charger utilization, 8.8 chargers/station, cars-per-capita 0.94/0.83/0.60 by region
Reusing the Problem-1 optimization skeleton and only adding the new shortest-path objective/constraint for the Korea urban model (modular model reuse instead of rebuilding)
Choosing Mahalanobis over Minkowski distance with an explicit justification (correlation/dimension invariance) to show statistical sophistication
Significance-filtering predictors via goodness-of-fit (deviance/Pearson) and Wald chi-square p-values, then keeping only the three that pass (WD, PO, GI) to derive the 'key factors'

Sensitivity analysis sweeping the two most influential parameters (chargers-per-station NAV 8.6-9.0, daily supply time AST 6-10 hrs) and tabulating the resulting station-count change to argue robustness
MATLAB geographic visualization (worldmap/geoshow/shaperead) to render choropleth density maps, the station connectivity network, and the convenient-area map
why_it_won: "Judge view: the paper wins on breadth-with-coherence and completeness rather than mathematical depth. It answers all five sub-questions fully (many teams run out of time on 4 and 5), and it ties them together with one consistent backbone (the urban/suburban/rural density split + a reusable optimization core). It is heavily data-anchored: real Tesla coordinates, Census density, GM utilization, Supercharger growth - giving concrete, defensible numbers (1.7M stations for the US, 67:23:10 urban:suburban:rural split, ~270k stations and a 2024-2069 adoption timeline for Korea) instead of hand-waving. It layers multiple recognizable OR/statistics tools (queuing, multi-objective IP, network shortest-path, logistic regression, Mahalanobis clustering, logistic growth) and signals method awareness by justifying choices (Mahalanobis vs Minkowski; congestion index to fix the clustering-downtown failure mode of naive shortest-path). It distills every part into a small set of actionable 'key factors' (wealth distribution, policy orientation, government investment) and converts them into a clean, leader-facing handout with country tiers - exactly the decision-relevant synthesis ICM judges reward. Innovation flags (custom connectivity/convenience/lag indices) plus sensitivity analysis and a working code appendix complete the package."
section_highlights: "Strong: the Summary Sheet leads with concrete numeric answers per question (1.7M stations, 67:23:10 split, key factors named) - judge-friendly. Consistent narrative spine (density tri-split) threaded through Q1-Q5. Explicit Assumptions-and-Justifications section. Modular write-up where Korea's model is presented as an extension of the US model ('similar to task one, we will not elaborate'). Named indices give the reader memorable hooks. Dedicated Strengths/Weaknesses/Extension section plus a real sensitivity table and a full MATLAB+SAS appendix. The one-page handout cleanly segments developed / developing / underdeveloped countries with tailored advice. Visuals (density choropleth, connectivity network, convenient-area map, timeline) carry the argument. Weak spots in presentation: several equations are typo-corrupted (CI constraint denominators inconsistent between sections), some figures are clearly templated/placeholder, and the lag-index section (4.5b) is mathematically muddled and hard to follow."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many quantitative inputs are weakly sourced or assumed (35% utilization, 8.8 chargers/station, cars-per-capita, EV penetration P), and headline outputs like '1.7M US stations' and '270k Korea stations' are presented without uncertainty bounds beyond a narrow 2-parameter sweep

M/M/1 queuing is a strong idealization: it assumes a single server per station, Poisson arrivals, exponential service, and steady state - ignoring multiple chargers per station (contradicting the 8.8-chargers assumption), time-of-day peaking, and trip-chaining
The 'connectivity rate 71%' and 'convenient area covers the whole US' conclusions are largely descriptive/visual and don't rigorously establish feasibility of full electrification

The logistic-regression 'key factors' rest on a tiny, opaque dataset (deviance df=35) and several reported p-values look internally inconsistent (e.g., predictors with high Wald chi-square but large p), so the WD/PO/GI selection is shaky
Q-type clustering uses only 8 countries and 5 indicators with single-linkage (chaining-prone) - too small to justify a general 3-tier classification of all nations
The lag-index construction (Section 4.5b) is under-specified and the proportionality argument (epsilon ~ x vs epsilon ~ -x) is confusing and not rigorously derived
The rural triangular-mesh coverage assumes idealized geometry and ignores actual road topology; suburban model collapses to a 1-D corridor
Stated weaknesses are honest but thin ('model is a bit rough'; ignores transition time)
Some data tables in the source render as obviously corrupted/templated values, suggesting figures were partly illustrative rather than computed
reusable_patterns: Geographic stratification spine: split a country by population density into urban/suburban/rural and reuse that partition across every sub-question for coherence and tractability

Define and name custom diagnostic indices (connectivity rate, convenience index, lag index) to convert vague problem language into computable, defensible quantities - a reliable ICM 'innovation' signal
Build one core multi-objective optimization model early, then extend it per scenario by adding a single objective/constraint rather than rebuilding (US -> Korea-urban shortest-path add-on)

Pair a capacity/service model (queuing M/M/1) with a siting/coverage model (network shortest-path + congestion re-weighting; triangular-mesh packing) to answer both 'how many' and 'where' in one workflow
Fix the 'shortest-path clusters everything downtown' failure mode with an iterative congestion-index edge re-weighting - a transferable trick for any facility-location problem that must spread out
Use logistic/logit regression as a key-factor screen: throw in candidate drivers, keep only those passing goodness-of-fit + Wald tests, and report the survivors as 'the factors that matter'
Justify your distance/linkage choice explicitly in clustering (Mahalanobis vs Minkowski) to demonstrate method awareness rather than defaulting to Euclidean
Anchor every parameter to a citable real source (scraped vendor data, census, industry utilization stats) and show the scraping code in the appendix to boost credibility
Run a focused sensitivity sweep on the 1-2 parameters that most drive the headline number and tabulate the output to claim robustness
Close with a decision-maker artifact (tiered handout: developed/developing/underdeveloped) that maps the model's key factors directly to recommended actions
